---
title: "From Individual Equations to Matrix Form"
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    incremental: true
---



## Step 3: Write the Model for Each Observation

We are fitting a simple linear regression:

\[
y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x
\]

---

### For each data point:

When \( x = 1 \):

\[
y_1 = \beta_0 \cdot 1 + \beta_1 \cdot 1
\]

When \( x = 2 \):

\[
y_2 = \beta_0 \cdot 1 + \beta_1 \cdot 2
\]

When \( x = 3 \):

\[
y_3 = \beta_0 \cdot 1 + \beta_1 \cdot 3
\]

---

## Step 4: Stack into Matrix Form

We can stack these equations:

\[
\begin{bmatrix}
3 \\
5 \\
7
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 1 \\
1 & 2 \\
1 & 3
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\beta_0 \\
\beta_1
\end{bmatrix}
\]

---

## What Just Happened?

We rewrote:

Three separate equations  

as  

One matrix equation:

\[
\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}
\]

---

## The Design Matrix Columns

The columns of the design matrix are basis functions:

- First column → \( b_0(x) = 1 \)
- Second column → \( b_1(x) = x \)

---

## Why This Matters

Linear regression is just:

> A weighted sum of basis functions.

\[
y = \beta_0 b_0(x) + \beta_1 b_1(x)
\]

This idea extends directly to GAMs —  
we simply add more (smoother) basis functions.

\
